result:
Some mornings, the world feels like a library before opening: lights humming, air holding its breath, spines waiting for fingertips. I put coffee on and think about the shelves we carry inside us—rows of almost-remembered afternoons, dog-eared moments, references to people who no longer answer their names. The kettle begins its thin whistle and I picture the index shifting: today under T for Tide, Tomorrow, and Trying Again.

Outside, the street performs its quiet theater. A cyclist draws a silver line through a puddle. A newspaper lands like a soft gavel on someone else’s day. Clouds argue, then decide to hold their peace. Everything is undecided and therefore generous.

I take the first sip and it is both bitter and forgiving. Maybe that is the definition of morning: a permission slip signed by heat. I think of the smallest infrastructures that keep us going—elastic bands around mail, the geometry of egg cartons, the uncelebrated heroism of zippers. All the modest inventions that practice reliability so we don’t have to be brave all at once.

By noon the library opens. You can’t hear it, but you can feel the card catalog of choices sliding open. Pick a subject: patience, repair, detours, laughter after disappointment. Check something out. Carry it with you for a while. Return it changed, and change it by returning.

By evening, I will misplace my keys and find instead a phrase I needed. The night desk lamp will circle a small moon on the table. I keep reading.

I put coffee on and think about the shelves we carry inside us—rows of almost-remembered afternoons, dog-eared moments, references to people who no longer answer their names. Outside, the street performs its quiet theater. A cyclist draws a silver line through a puddle. A newspaper lands like a soft gavel on someone else’s day. Clouds argue, then decide to hold their peace. Everything is undecided and therefore generous.

I take the first sip and it is both bitter and forgiving. Maybe that is the definition of morning: a permission slip signed by heat. I think of the smallest infrastructures that keep us going—elastic bands around mail, the geometry of egg cartons, the uncelebrated heroism of zippers. All the modest inventions that practice reliability so we don’t have to be brave all at once.

By noon the library opens. You can’t hear it, but you can feel the card catalog of choices sliding open. Pick a subject: patience, repair, detours, laughter after disappointment. Check something out. Carry it with you for a while. Return it changed, and change it by returning.

By evening, I will misplace my keys and find instead a phrase I needed. The night desk lamp will circle a small moon on the table. I keep reading.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
